title: MeshCore vs Meshtastic: Why I Am Staying with Meshtastic
content: Over the last few months, I have been testing two popular LoRa-based decentralized communication systems: Meshtastic and MeshCore.

I carried a node around the city, tested indoors and outdoors, and set up a home station with a large balcony antenna. I also joined both communities to understand what would actually be useful in day-to-day life.
My goal was simple: practical communication with friends and a usable emergency backup channel.
After testing both systems, I am staying with Meshtastic.
Meshtastic worked better than I first realized. Messages went through from my apartment, elevator, streets, and other places where I expected weak signal.
My initial frustration with Meshtastic was not technical. Public channels were often quiet, and that can make a healthy network feel dead to newcomers.
Then I tried MeshCore. In the Netherlands, it felt alive immediately. I started receiving messages right away and saw much more active conversation.
That excitement faded once I used it in normal conditions. The practical problem was infrastructure dependence.
With Meshtastic, each node can help relay messages. With MeshCore, your experience depends more on dedicated infrastructure.
In theory that can be fine, but in practice it changes everything. Infrastructure must be built and maintained by enthusiasts, and reliability varies.
At home, with a big antenna, MeshCore worked. Near repeaters, it could also work. Walking around with a small pocket node was much less reliable.
If the device stayed in a bag or pocket, many outgoing messages failed. To improve odds, I often had to hold it high in the air, which is not practical.
In real use, transmission reliability was not good enough for my needs.
Meshtastic was less dramatic. I flashed, configured, and used it. No constant dependence on nearby repeater infrastructure.
I could keep the node indoors or carry it around and still get reasonable delivery rates without special handling.
Another concern is social dynamics. Infrastructure-heavy networks give more influence to the people running core repeaters.
That naturally introduces coordination politics around settings, channels, and regional decisions. Some governance is necessary, but this shifts the balance.
For my use case, I want a system where personal nodes remain useful without depending on local infrastructure maintainers.
To be fair, MeshCore's approach has valid technical arguments around structure and potential scalability, especially in dense environments.
But for me, a theoretically better architecture is not enough if a pocket node cannot reliably send while moving through the city.
My conclusion is simple: MeshCore felt more alive, Meshtastic felt more usable, and usability wins for my goals.
Meshtastic is not perfect. It can be quiet and may face congestion at scale. Still, its node-first model matches what decentralized communication should feel like.
You turn on a node, and it becomes part of the network.
